Title: Our thoughts
ChapterText: The thoughts can affect our emotions, our behavior and in the end the life we live. The thoughts of people with social anxiety tend to be overly negative, untruthful and self-criticizing.

Title: Automatic thoughts
ChapterText:
Automatic thoughts are thoughts that appear in the mind automatically without any effort. People suffering social anxiety tend to have many negative automatic thoughts. Thoughts like:"I will get embarrased" or "I look terrible" can appear in a matter of milliseconds and can profoundly affect the way we feel and behave.
Thought like "I look hideous" can result in escape from the social situation.

Title: Cognitive restructuring
ChapterText:
Imagine your thoughts are like a pair of glasses. If the lenses are distorted, the whole world looks blurry or scary. Cognitive Restructuring is the core tool of CBT(Cognitive Behavioral Therapy) that helps you clean the lenses.
It is a systematic process of identifying, challenging, and changing the unhelpful thinking patterns that fuel anxiety and low mood.

Title: Cognitive restructuring process
ChapterText:
The process of cognitive restructuring involves:

1)Catch it
Notice when you feel a sudden drop in mood or a spike in anxiety. What was just running through your mind? (e.g., Everyone thinks I'm boring.

2) Check it: Act like a detective. Look for evidence
Is this a fact or just a feeling?
Am I predicting the future or "reading" minds?
What would I say to a friend in this situation?

3)Change it
Replace the original thought with a balanced alternative. This isn't happy thinking it's realistic thinking. (e.g.,I don't know what they are thinking, but some people stayed to talk to me, so I’m doing okay

Title: Why it works
ChapterText:
By repeatedly questioning your "automatic" thoughts, you weaken the brain's old, anxious pathways. Over time, your brain stops jumping to the worst-case scenario and starts looking at the facts first.

Remember: You don't have to believe everything you think.
